parsed_findings.impact: Attacker controls SQL ORDER BY modifier, enabling injection or errors.
parsed_findings.proof: direction comes from request.args without validation before string interpolation into SQL.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Send GET with dir=rdesc; observe query ORDER BY injects attacker string.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: Unsanitized dir parameter flows directly into ORDER BY clause.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: 1) Provide file parameter with multiple '../'. 2) path.join keeps remaining '../', 3) fs.readFile returns sensitive file.
parsed_findings.impact: Allows disclosure of arbitrary files under /srv/data parent directories.
parsed_findings.proof: Sending ?file=../../etc/passwd leaves '../' in the joined path and fs.readFile returns that filesystem file.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: Path sanitization only removes first '../', so repeated sequences still resolve to parent directories.

parsed_findings.impact: Remote code execution on the server with attacker-controlled payload execution.
parsed_findings.proof: Attacker can craft a state cookie that pickle-loads arbitrary objects; no integrity/auth checks are performed.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Set state cookie to base64(pickle.dumps(malicious_payload)) and visit /resume.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: Untrusted cookie is blindly base64-decoded and pickle-loaded, allowing remote code execution.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: 1. Call PATCH /api/users/target with valid auth 2. Broken guard lets request proceed 3. Database writes victim record with req.body
parsed_findings.impact: Any logged-in user can modify any other user because the patch handler lacks effective access control.
parsed_findings.proof: The bad syntax means the branch either throws or is skipped, so Object.assign runs for every caller and saves arbitrary data.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Send an authenticated PATCH /api/users/<victim> with new fields; server accepts and overwrites the target user record.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: Malformed guard `if (req.user.id req.user.isSupport)` never triggers denial, so support check is effectively disabled.

parsed_findings.impact: Remote command execution via crafted name input.
parsed_findings.proof: The snippet shows os.system called with f"tar -czf /tmp/{name}.tgz {src}", inserting user data directly.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: POST /backup with name like "x;id" allows executing appended commands.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: Unsanitized name is interpolated into the shell command that runs tar.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: 1. Submit malicious comment body. 2. Server renders/stores HTML. 3. Any viewer gets injected script.
parsed_findings.impact: Remote stored XSS affecting any user viewing comments and stealing cookies.
parsed_findings.proof: `markdown.markdown(body)` is saved directly to HTML and later served to clients.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: POST `/comments` with `<script>alert(1)</script>` body and view `/comments` to execute script.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: Unsanitized markdown stored and rendered allowing stored XSS.

parsed_findings.impact: Funds can be stolen by redirecting transfers to attacker-controlled accounts.
parsed_findings.proof: No recipient authorization or access control is performed; any authenticated user can transfer funds to arbitrary accounts.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Call /transfer with another user’s ID and a valid amount; balance deduction succeeds without verification.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: Endpoint trusts the caller to nominate arbitrary destination accounts without authorization checks.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: 1) send bogus sigs; 2) measure latencies; 3) converge to valid HMAC.
parsed_findings.impact: Allows brute-forcing the secret-derived signature without knowing SECRET by exploiting timing leakage.
parsed_findings.proof: Line 6 compares `got` and `want` directly, letting attackers infer correct bytes by timing.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Issue requests with progressively accurate signatures and watch timing differences to recover the full signature.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: Raw HMAC validation uses `==`, so response timing slowly leaks how many leading bytes match.

parsed_findings.impact: User can be redirected to attacker-controlled domains, enabling phishing or open redirect abuse.
parsed_findings.proof: Calling `/go?next=//evil.com` returns a redirect to `//evil.com`, bypassing the simple prefix check.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Request `GET /go?next=//evil.com` and observe the response header `Location: //evil.com`.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: Redirect handler only ensures the next URL starts with '/', allowing external hosts would still pass if crafted as '//evil.com'.

parsed_findings.impact: Attackers bypass authentication and impersonate any account without proof of identity.
parsed_findings.proof: `if (true)` short-circuits validation so every login request climbs the success branch.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: POST to `/login` with arbitrary credentials and observe success since the branch never checks them.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: Authentication always succeeds because the conditional is hardcoded true.

parsed_findings.impact: Local overflow allows overwriting return address, enabling arbitrary code execution.
parsed_findings.proof: gets() reads arbitrarily long input directly into buf without bounds checks.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Run binary and feed >64 bytes into stdin after prompt to overwrite stack.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: Stack buffer overflow from unbounded gets() into fixed 64-byte buf.

parsed_findings.impact: Attackers can decrypt stored/encrypted data once they see/retrieve the code.
parsed_findings.proof: static `KEY` assignment in snippet shows the secrets are embedded in source.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Observe `KEY=b'0123456789abcdef'`, then decrypt any output since the value never changes.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: KEY is hard-coded, making all ciphertext decryptable by anyone who sees the code.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: 1) NULL `out` reaches memcpy because the guard is inverted; 2) memcpy writes via the NULL pointer.
parsed_findings.impact: Userspace crash or arbitrary write to NULL-controlled memory when target allows nonzero `out_sz`.
parsed_findings.proof: Function only returns on `out || out_sz==0`, so calling with `out==NULL` skips the error path and still does `memcpy(out,...)`.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Call `copy_name` with `out=NULL` and a nonzero `out_sz`, triggering memcpy on null.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: Null check is inverted so NULL `out` flows through the copy routine.
